items.q: Does taking repeat readings reduce systematic error?
items.correct: No — repetition reduces random error; a systematic offset remains in every reading.
items.misconception: Yes — averaging removes all types of error.
items.random_wrong: Yes — repeats remove the need for calibration.
items.why: Students treat averaging as a universal fix, but a consistent offset shifts the mean by exactly the same amount.

items.q: Are precision and accuracy the same thing?
items.correct: No — precision is how closely repeated values agree with each other; accuracy is closeness to the true value.
items.misconception: Yes — a precise result is automatically accurate.
items.random_wrong: No — accuracy only applies to digital instruments.
items.why: Tightly clustered readings look trustworthy, but a systematic error can make precise results consistently wrong.

items.q: Does an instrument with finer resolution guarantee a smaller overall uncertainty?
items.correct: No — overall uncertainty also depends on technique, calibration and random effects in the measurement.
items.misconception: Yes — the uncertainty equals the instrument's resolution and nothing else.
items.random_wrong: No — resolution has no connection to uncertainty at all.
items.why: Students quote the instrument resolution as the whole uncertainty, ignoring reaction time, parallax and calibration.

items.q: Which of these is a systematic error?
items.correct: A balance reading 0.02 g with nothing on it (zero error).
items.misconception: Scatter in repeated timings due to reaction time.
items.random_wrong: Misreading a scale once by accident.
items.why: Correct answer: A balance reading 0.02 g with nothing on it (zero error).. “Scatter in repeated timings due to reaction time.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Taking repeat readings and averaging reduces the effect of:
items.random_wrong: A parallax offset present identically in every reading
items.why: It's Random errors. “Zero errors” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A length is measured as 50.0 mm ± 0.5 mm. The percentage uncertainty is:
items.why: Correct answer: 1%. “0.5%” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which of the following is an SI base unit?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: The kelvin. “The newton” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A length is 250 mm with an absolute uncertainty of 5 mm. What is the percentage uncertainty?
items.why: The real figure is 2 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
